The program is an outgrowth of the effort begun over 20 years ago in the Aircraft Nuclear Propulsion (ANP) Program to make a molten-salt reactor power plant for aircraft.
A molten-salt reactor, the Aircraft Reactor Experiment, was operated at Oak Ridge National Laboratory in 1954 as part of the ANP Program.
Fuel for this type of reactor would be $^{233}\mathrm{UF}_4$ dissolved in a mixture of LiF and $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ , but $^{235}\mathrm{U}$ or plutonium could be used for startup.
A major program activity through 1969 was the operation of the Molten-Salt Reactor Experiment (MSRE).
The MSRE operated at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and produced $7.3\mathrm{MW}$ of heat.
The initial fuel contained $0.9\mathrm{mole}\% \mathrm{UF}_4,5\% \mathrm{ZrF}_4,$ $29\% \mathrm{BeF}_2$ ,and $65\%$ LiF; the uranium was about $33\%$ ${}^{235}\mathrm{U}$ .

The conclusion was that this objective had been achieved and that the MSRE had shown that molten-fluoride reactors can be operated at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ without corrosive attack on either the metal or graphite parts of the system; also the fuel is stable; the reactor equipment can operate satisfactorily at these conditions; xenon can be removed rapidly from molten salts; and when necessary, the radioactive equipment can be repaired or replaced.

The second phase of MSRE operation began in August 1968, when a small facility in the MSRE building was used to remove the original uranium charge from the fuel salt by treatment with gaseous $\mathbf{F}_2$ .
In six days of fluorination, $221\mathrm{kg}$ of uranium was removed from the molten salt and loaded into absorbers filled with sodium fluoride pellets.
After the fuel was processed, a charge of $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ was added to the original carrier salt, and in October 1968 the MSRE became the world's first reactor to operate on $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
The nuclear characteristics of the MSRE with the $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ were close to the predictions, and the reactor was quite stable.
In September 1969, small amounts of $\mathrm{PuF}_3$ were added to the fuel to obtain some experience with plutonium in a molten-salt reactor.
The MSRE was shut down permanently December 12, 1969, so that the funds supporting its operation could be used elsewhere in the research and development program.

However, in late 1967 a one-fluid breeder became feasible with the development of processes that use liquid bismuth to isolate protactinium and remove rare earths from a salt that also contains thorium.

In the congressional authorization report on the AEC's programs for FY 1973, the Joint Committee on Atomic Energy recommended that the molten-salt reactor be appraised so that a decision could be made about its continuation and the level of funding appropriate for it.
A significant result of the review was the preparation of ORNL-4812, The Development Status of Molten-Salt Breeder Reactors.
A subsequent decision was made by the AEC to terminate work on molten-salt reactors for budgetary reasons; in January 1973 ORNL was directed to conclude MSR development work.
In January 1974, the AEC program for molten-salt reactor development was reinstated.
A considerable effort during 1974 was concerned with assembling a program staff, making operational a number of development facilities used previously, and replacing

A significant undertaking was the formulation of detailed plans for the development of molten-salt breeder reactors and the preparation of ORNL-5018, Program Plan for Development of Molten-Salt Breeder Reactors.
During 1974 and 1975, work in the Molten-Salt Reactor Program was devoted to the technology needed for molten-salt reactors.
These studies culminated in the demonstration of an adequate basis for management of tritium in a 1000-Mw(e) MSBR.

The results, in conjunction with the measured concentrations of combined tritium in the salt, indicated that the ratio of combined-to-elemental tritium in the salt was about 300 to 500.

The overall material balance on tritium flow rate appeared to be between 0.89 and 0.97.

Measurements of the partial pressure of hydrogen in the salt $(0.43\mathrm{Pa})$ were reasonably consistent with the apparent partial pressure of elemental hydrogen in the pump-bowl gas space, as indicated by the observed concentration of elemental tritium in off-gas samples.
Use of the measured partial pressure with the measured tritium concentration in the salt gives a value of 4700 for the ratio of combined-to-elemental tritium in the salt.
If this concentration ratio were attained in the reference-design MSBR, only $\sim 3.5\mathrm{Ci / d}$ of tritium would be released to the steam system and, hence, to the environment.
The development of a 123-energy-group neutron cross-section library, based on ENDF/B, Version IV, was completed.
The library includes cross sections at four temperatures from $300\mathrm{K}$ to $1200\mathrm{K}$ for several fuel-moderator ratios that are appropriate to the reference-design MSBR.

While the work required to complete the cross-section processing and the performance calculations has been identified, it will not be done because the MSR program will be terminated at the end of FY 1976.

The estimated helium concentration in the Hastelloy N pressure vessel of an MSBR after 30 full-power years, due principally to two-step ${}^{58}\mathrm{Ni}(n,\gamma)$ ${}^{59}\mathrm{Ni}(n,\alpha)$ reactions, is 38 ppm at the inner surface.

The calculated steady-state concentration of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ in the uranium in an MSBR is 20 ppm if protactinium is removed from the salt on a 10-day cycle and if no $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ is present.

(About 15 years would be required to approach equilibrium.) Approximately 27% of the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ results from $(n,2n)$ and $(\gamma ,n)$ reactions in $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ , and the remainder is due to similar reactions in $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ .
A concentration of 23 ppm $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ in thorium would double the steady-state concentration of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ .
Because of the termination of the MSR Program at the end of FY 1976, the loop has been drained and put in standby condition.

The forced-convection loop, MSR-FCL-2b, has accumulated 4000 hr of operation with MSBR reference fuel salt at design conditions $(566^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ minimum, $704^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ maximum; 2.5 and $5.0\mathrm{m / s}$ salt velocities), with the expected low corrosion rates in standard Hastelloy N.
Additions of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ were made in preparation for determining the corrosion rates for this alloy at higher $\mathrm{U}^{4 + } / \mathrm{U}^{3 + }$ ratios.

Because of the decision to terminate the MSR Program at the end of FY 1976, the corrosion studies with standard Hastelloy N were discontinued; tests are now in progress to examine the corrosion of Hastelloy N modified by the addition of $1\%$ niobium.

Measurements were made of the solubility of $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ in molten $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{BeF}_4$ over the range from 500 to $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ . $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ was prepared using a mixture of stable tellurium and $^{123m}\mathrm{Te}$ tracer.
The most recent data indicate a solubility below $10^{-5}$ mole fraction at $700^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

This work has progressed to the point where three tentative conclusions can be made: (1) $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ is quite insoluble in $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ melts; (2) the light-absorbing species in $\mathrm{LiF - BeF_2}$ melts apparently can be represented as $\mathrm{Te}_n^{n-}$ ; (3) although the results are not conclusive, it is reasonable to expect the $\mathrm{Te}_3^-$ ion to exist in molten-salt solutions.

In other spectral studies, the pressures of tellurium due to the equilibrium $2\mathrm{LiTe}_3 = \mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te} + {}^5 /_2\mathrm{Te}_2(\mathrm{g})$ were determined over the range from 500 to $750^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The measured equilibrium pressures of $\mathrm{Te}_2$ over $\mathrm{LiTe}_3$ were only about a factor of 1.5 lower than the vapor pressures of $\mathrm{Te}_2$ and can be represented by the equation $\ln P(\mathrm{mmHg}) = 15.938 - 12,720 / T(\mathrm{K})$ .
Evidence for a slightly volatile lithium telluride was found at temperatures around $1000^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Electrochemical studies were initiated on the lithium tellurides $\mathrm{Li}_2\mathrm{Te}$ and $\mathrm{LiTe}_3$ in LiCl-KCl eutectic at $400^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Studies of the equilibrium $\mathrm{UF_4(d) + \Omega^1 / 2H_2(g) =}$ $\mathrm{UF}_3(\mathrm{d}) + \mathrm{HF}(\mathrm{g})$ in molten LiF-BeF $_2$ mixtures were continued using a spectroscopic method.
Initially, the molar extinction coefficient for $\mathrm{UF}_3$ was determined in LiF-BeF $_2$ (66-34 and 48-52 mole $\%$ ).
Values of the quotient for the above equilibrium were then determined in the two solvents over the range from 500 to $800^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The quotients in the 66-34 mole $\%$ solvent were essentially the same as those measured previously by Long and Blankenship; however, those in the 48-52 mole $\%$ solvent were about 10 times greater than those determined by Long and Blankenship.

Raman and $^{19}\mathrm{F}$ NMR spectroscopy were used to study the hydrolytic behavior of $\mathrm{Na}_3\mathrm{B}_3\mathrm{F}_6\mathrm{O}_3$ since this compound appears to be the stable oxygen-containing species in NaF-NaBF $_4$ (8-92 mole %) when the total oxygen concentration is low.
At temperatures above $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , $\mathrm{BF}_3\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ is completely dissociated.
Below $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , association in the vapor phase becomes pronounced, and, with sufficient $\mathrm{BF}_3\cdot 2\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{O}$ in the system, a stable liquid phase is formed.
The vapor pressure of the liquid reaches 1 atm at about $200^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

text: Uranium-232 has a decay chain that approaches equilibrium over a several-year period; the nuclides in this chain emit high-energy $\alpha$ -alpha particles and gamma rays.
As a result, shielding and handling costs of reactor fuels increase as the $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ content increases.

Increasing the content of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ in fuels of conventional reactors for which reprocessing and refabrication are required raises the cost by which these processes are performed and lowers the value of the discharged fuel.
In the absence of light nuclides which can undergo $(\alpha,n)$ reactions, the principal radiations from the standpoint of shielding are the 2.6-MeV gamma from $^{208}\mathrm{Tl}$ and the
2.2-MeV gamma from $^{212}\mathrm{Bi}$ .
The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ production in the reference design MSBR was examined to determine the concentration of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ that would be present in the MSBR.

Decay of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ is not a direct problem for fuel used in the MSBR (because no fabrication is required), but the content of this isotope in excess bred fuel could affect handling and shipping costs.
The five processes by which $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ can be produced are

For the $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ : $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ ratios ( $\sim 56$ ) expected in the MSBR, the $(n,2n)$ reaction in $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ is more important than the $(n,2n)$ reaction in $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .
However, removal of the protactinium isotopes by fuel processing on a 10-day cycle greatly reduces the production of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ from $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ reactions.
As a consequence, the net production rates of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ from the two $(n,2n)$ processes may be comparable in an MSBR.

The $^{230}\mathrm{Th}(n,\gamma)$ cross section was based on a 2200 m/sec value of 22.7 b $(22.7\times 10^{-28}\mathrm{m}^2)$ and a resonance integral of 996 b $(966\times 10^{-28}\mathrm{m}^2)$ .

The $(\gamma ,n)$ cross sections for both $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ and $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ were taken as 0.05 b $(0.05\times 10^{-28}\mathrm{m}^2)$ for gamma energies above 6.0 MeV and zero for gamma energies below 6.0 MeV.

Solution of this expression for the reference design 1000 MW(e) MSBR with a ten-day processing cycle for protactinium removal and no $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ leads to an equilibrium concentration of 20 ppm for $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ in total uranium.
Of this amount, $27\%$ is produced by reactions in $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ .

The $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ source from $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ would about equal the combined source from $^{232}\mathrm{Th}$ and $^{233}\mathrm{U}$ at a concentration of 23 ppm $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ in thorium.
Thorium-230, which is a decay product of $^{234}\mathrm{U}$ , is present to about 17 ppm in natural uranium.
Thus, if the thorium to be used in a reactor is obtained from mineral deposits that also contain uranium, such thorium could contain a significant amount of $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ .
The Th:U ratio in Monazite sands, a major thorium resource, is typically about 20.
Hence, thorium from such deposits could be expected to contain only about 1 ppm $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ an insignificant concentration from the standpoint of $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ production.

However, in the absence of a large thorium industry, initial demands for thorium conceivably could be met by material recovered from uranium mill tailings, where the Th:U ratio in the raw ore could have been 0.2 or less.

(The uranium ores at Blind River in Canada, for example, have Th:U ratios of about 0.2.) Hence the $^{230}\mathrm{Th}$ content could exceed 100 ppm, which would raise the equilibrium $^{232}\mathrm{U}$ concentration to about 107 ppm in total uranium in an MSBR.

At this time, the loop has operated approximately 4000 hr with fuel salt at MSBR reference design conditions with the expected low corrosion rates in the standard alloy.
A test has been started to obtain corrosion data on specimens of Hastelloy N containing $1\%$ niobium.
A 4000-hr corrosion test was completed on October 2, 1975, in which standard Hastelloy N
During the test, 18 corrosion specimens were exposed to salt at velocities up to $5\mathrm{m / sec}$ at three different temperature levels-565, 635, and $705^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
As expected, corrosion rates were low throughout the test period and $< 3\mu \mathrm{m}/$ year (0.1 mil/year) during the final 1000-hr increment of the test.
On October 6, a series of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ additions to the loop salt was begun to increase the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio.

It was planned to increase the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio from the earlier equilibrium value of about 100 to a new level of about 1000 and then to evaluate corrosion rates at the higher level, which reflects a more oxidizing condition in the salt.

The $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ was added by dropping small pellets of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ -fuel carrier salt (50-50 wt%) from a sealed air lock, via a ball valve and riser pipe, to the fuel-salt surface within the auxiliary pump tank.
During the additions, the loop was operated isothermally at $\sim 695^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with a pump speed of $4000~\mathrm{rpm}$ to provide a salt flow rate of about 12 liters/min.

Four separate additions of $0.8\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ were made between October 6 and October 21, followed by a final addition of $1.6\mathrm{g}$ on November 3, 1975 ( $4.8\mathrm{g}$ total addition to the $14\mathrm{-kg}$ salt inventory).
The effects of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ additions were similar to those observed in September and October 1974, when fine $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ powder was added to the salt inventory of FCL-2b.
Immediate pump-power increases of 8 to $10\%$ were again noted with each $0.8\mathrm{-g}$ addition.
During the first four $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ additions of $0.8\mathrm{g}$ each, the salt level dropped about $1^{1/2}\mathrm{mm}$ immediately after each addition.

A $1^{1/2}\mathrm{mm}$ level change represents an apparent salt density increase of about $1\%$ ; therefore, density change of the pumped salt should account for only about one-tenth of the pump-power increases noted, and the remaining power increase is believed to be due to increased drag forces on the pump impeller.
The final addition of $1.6\mathrm{g}$ of $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ resulted in a $14\%$ increase in pump power and a larger-than-expected level drop of $12\mathrm{mm}$ at the auxiliary tank.

Each $\mathrm{NiF}_2$ addition to the salt resulted in an immediate increase in the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio, which reached a maximum in a few hours, followed by asymptotic decay; e.g., the first addition increased
the ratio from $\sim 100$ to $>1000$ , but after three days of isothermal operation at $695^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the ratio had decreased to 130.
Similarly, the fourth addition increased the ratio from $\sim 250$ to 10,000, but it had dropped to 200 after eight days.
Just prior to the final $1.6 - \mathrm{g} \mathrm{NiF}_2$ addition on November 3, the $\mathrm{U}^{4+} / \mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio was 150.
Two hours after the 1.6-g addition, the ratio was $2.6 \times 10^4$ , and a day later the ratio had fallen to $4.8 \times 10^3$ .

A modified Hastelloy N containing $2\%$ titanium has good resistance to irradiation embrittlement, but does not have sufficient resistance to shallow intergranular cracking by tellurium.
An alloy that contains $2\%$ titanium appears to adequately resist irradiation embrittlement, but the $2\%$ titanium does not impart adequate resistance to intergranular embrittlement by tellurium.

Additions of 0.5 to $2\%$ niobium are effective in reducing tellurium-induced shallow intergranular cracking, but the effects of neutron irradiation on alloys containing niobium have not been characterized fully.
Increasing the chromium concentration from the present $7\%$ to between 12 and $15\%$ may also be beneficial in preventing tellurium-induced shallow intergranular attack.

Currently, factors associated with production of the $2\%$ titanium-modified alloy in commercial quantities are being studied, while smaller heats are being made of Hastelloy N containing additions of niobium alone as well as additions of niobium and rare earths to $2\%$ titanium-modified Hastelloy N.
Two large heats, one 10,000 lb and the other 8,000 lb, of the $2\%$ titanium-modified alloy have been melted by a commercial vendor.
One 2500-lb heat of an alloy modified with $2\%$ titanium- $1\%$ niobium was produced and converted to bar products.

Thermal convection loop 23 is constructed of Inconel 601, a material that has shown good resistance to grain boundary attack by tellurium.
However, results have shown that Inconel 601 undergoes extensive corrosion in fuel salt, making it a material with little prospect for use in an MSBR.

Because of the extensive corrosion which this alloy has undergone by reaction with fuel salt, the $\mathrm{U}^{4+}/\mathrm{U}^{3+}$ ratio of the salt has fallen to about 4, the most reducing level ever achieved in a loop.

As reported previously<sup>10</sup> it was decided to use this very reducing salt to try to reproduce the results of Toth and Gilpatrick,<sup>11</sup> which predict that under the operating conditions of this loop and in the presence of graphite, $\mathrm{UC}_2$ would be formed by the reaction
4 \mathrm {U F} _ {3} + 2 \mathrm {C} \rightharpoonup 3 \mathrm {U F} + \mathrm {U C} _ {2}.
In the first attempt to produce this reaction, pyrolytic graphite was exposed to the salt for 500 hr, and no $\mathrm{UC}_2$ was found.
A third set of graphite specimens which has been kept free of moisture is now undergoing a 2000-hr exposure and is scheduled to be examined in March 1976.
Sixteen Hastelloy N specimens have been exposed a total of 10,009 hr.

For the first $1000\mathrm{hr}$ of operation, this loop was used to gather baseline corrosion data with "as-received" salt.
